text.title: Solo MRR Dashboard Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for computing MRR, churn, ARPU, and LTV from Stripe in a single-operator SaaS without finance support.

rule.statement: Annual plans contribute (unit_amount / 12) to MRR, applied per month for the full 12 months of the term. Quarterly plans divide by 3. The full annual payment is NOT booked as a single month of MRR.
rule.rationale: Booking annual payments as one-month spikes inflates MRR in the signup month and tanks it the next, destroying churn-rate accuracy. Spreading matches the underlying revenue commitment.

rule.statement: Refunds in month M subtract from MRR in month M only. They do NOT retroactively delete the subscription from earlier months. Voids (mistaken charges, $0 net) are excluded entirely.
rule.rationale: Retroactive deletion makes prior-month numbers move every time you reopen the sheet — fatal for trust. Period-bound treatment keeps each month's number stable once closed.

rule.statement: On the 5th of each month, the prior month's MRR / churn / ARPU / LTV / customer-count are snapshotted into an append-only "closed months" tab. The closed snapshot is the canonical number — investor updates, dashboards, and analyses cite it, not a live recompute.
rule.rationale: Stripe's data continues to backfill (refunds, disputes) for weeks. A frozen snapshot prevents number drift after the month has been reported externally.

rule.statement: Each of the five metrics has exactly one formula stored next to the dashboard. Variants ("MRR ex-trials", "MRR ex-refunds") are forbidden — pick one definition. Gross churn = MRR lost from cancels + downgrades in month / MRR at start of month. Net churn allows expansions; the chosen definition is stated explicitly.
rule.rationale: Multiple "MRR" definitions destroy benchmarking against the operator's own past months and against industry comparables.

rule.statement: "Customer" in MRR-land is one paid subscription, not one auth user or one workspace. A workspace with three seats on one subscription is one customer. Per-seat plans count seats separately only inside an "average seats per customer" tile, never in the headline customer count.
rule.rationale: Solo operators routinely double-count customers by mixing auth-user counts with subscription counts. The discipline of one definition per dashboard line prevents headline-number inflation.

example.note: Annual prices divided by 12, refunds handled by a separate `monthly_refunds(on)` pass that subtracts at the month level, not retroactively.

antipattern.description: Operator reports "$8k MRR this month" by summing all annual payments collected this month plus monthly recurring — number triples in October and crashes in November.
antipattern.reason: Violates r1 (annual not spread) and erodes investor trust the first time MRR "drops 60% with no churn".
